title: Stop asking 'Which AI is the best?' Start asking 'Best at what?'
description: I run 6 different AI models in my daily workflow — each for a specific job. Here's why picking the right tool matters more than picking the 'best' tool.
content:
Every week someone asks me: 'Which AI model should I use?'

The honest answer? It depends on what you're trying to do. I stopped chasing the 'best' model a long time ago. Instead, I match the tool to the task.

My current stack

Claude — Long-form content, strategy docs, code architecture. Handles 100K+ token contexts without losing coherence.

ChatGPT — Quick drafts, ad copy variations, brainstorming sessions. Fast and good enough for first passes.

Gemini — SEO analysis, Google-specific content. It understands search intent better than any other model I've tested.

Perplexity — Research. Links sources, gives me real citations I can verify instead of hallucinated references.

Cursor — Code generation and debugging. Context-aware across my entire codebase.

Midjourney — Visual assets, social media graphics, blog headers.

The takeaway

Stop asking which AI is the best. Start asking: 'What am I trying to build, and which tool fits that job?' That shift alone changed how I work.

title: Google ranking was the game. AI recommendation is the new game.
description: Most websites aren't even on the field yet. Here's what AEO and GEO mean for your visibility strategy.
content:
You've heard the terms: AEO (Answer Engine Optimization) and GEO (Generative Engine Optimization). They're not buzzwords — they're the next frontier of search.

When someone asks ChatGPT or Gemini a question, it doesn't show a list of 10 blue links. It synthesizes an answer from multiple sources. If your content isn't structured for this, you're invisible.

What changes

Traditional SEO: Rank #1 for a keyword → get 30% of clicks.

AI-driven visibility: Get cited by 3 AI models → get referenced in 80% of relevant answers.

How to prepare

  • Write clear definitions. If your page answers 'What is X?' clearly, AI models will cite it.
  • Use structured data. Schema markup helps AI understand your content.
  • Build topical authority. Publish multiple pieces on related topics — AI models trust depth.
  • Optimize for conversational queries. People ask AI full sentences, not keywords.

The playbook is being rewritten. The good news? Most competitors aren't playing the new game yet.

title: Nobody explained LLMs.txt properly. So let me do it in plain English.
description: You know how robots.txt tells Google which pages to crawl — LLMs.txt does the same for AI models.
content:
You know robots.txt — the file that tells search engines which pages they can and can't crawl. LLMs.txt does the same thing for AI models.

It's a text file you place at the root of your domain. Inside, you define which content AI models can use for training and inference — and what they should ignore.

Why it matters

AI models scrape the web to train. Without an LLMs.txt file, you're giving them blanket permission by default. If you have proprietary content, pricing pages, or client data behind login walls, this matters.

Basic syntax

User-agent: GPTBot
Disallow: /dashboard/
Disallow: /pricing/

User-agent: Claude-Web
Allow: /blog/
Disallow: /private/

What every marketer should do

  • Create an LLMs.txt file at the root of your domain.
  • Allow your blog and public resources.
  • Disallow pricing pages, internal tools, and draft content.
  • Review it quarterly as new AI crawlers emerge.

Think of it as the new robots.txt. Ignore it at your own risk.
